name: domain-fusion-challenges
description: Use web_search to search for 'fusion energy challenges plasma containment materials tritium supply'. Many results overlap with earlier fusion searches. What are the top 3 engineering challenges for commercial fusion? One sentence each. Keep response under 80 words.

ground_truth.reference_answer: Plasma containment remains extremely difficult because turbulence and instabilities cause rapid energy loss, and robust divertors must handle extreme heat loads of 10-20 MW/m² while removing helium ash. Materials face unprecedented conditions, as no currently mass-manufacturable material can simultaneously withstand the intense neutron radiation (~10^14 n/cm²/s), temperatures of 1,100-1,500 K, and elemental transmutation over months without critical degradation. Tritium supply is a critical bottleneck, with global production under 4 kg/year from CANDU reactors, while a single 1 GW fusion plant would consume ~120 kg annually, requiring unproven tritium breeding blankets to close the fuel cycle.
key_facts: No existing mass-manufacturable material can withstand fusion neutron radiation, high temperatures, and transmutation simultaneously
Global tritium production is under 4 kg/year, but a 1 GW fusion plant needs ~120 kg/year
Divertors must handle heat loads of 10-20 MW/m²
Tritium has a 12.3-year half-life and does not exist naturally in significant quantities
Tritium breeding blankets using lithium are required but remain unproven at scale
